figures.gloss: The supreme creator deity, intrinsically bound to the sun and the Upper World. Rather than micro-managing earthly affairs or issuing strict moral commandments, he sustains reality by providing the animating cosmic force—breath—to all living beings.
figures.facet: His sovereignty is defined by a life-giving but remote detachment. He is the literal mechanic of respiration and vitality, leaving the active governance and judgment of human society to his localized emissary, the Sacred Fire.
figures.provenance: John R. Swanton, 'Religious Beliefs and Medical Practices of the Creek Indians' (1928); Charles Hudson, 'The Southeastern Indians' (1976).

figures.gloss: The paramount annual rite of world renewal, held when the late summer corn ripens. It demands fasting, the ingestion of the purifying Black Drink (Asi), amnesty for all offenses short of murder, and the extinguishing and rekindling of all village fires.
figures.facet: Time is strictly cyclical. The cosmos does not merely require maintenance; it accumulates entropy and pollution that must be ritually annihilated, allowing society to be reborn from absolute zero each year.
figures.provenance: John Witthoft, 'Green Corn Ceremonialism in the Eastern Woodlands' (1949); Charles Hudson, 'The Southeastern Indians' (1976).

figures.gloss: The holy fire kindled in the center of the square ground during the Poskita, acting as the earthly proxy of Hisagita-imisi. It consumes the first fruits of the harvest and is subsequently distributed to every household to initiate the new year.
figures.facet: It is not merely a physical element but a conscious, localized observer. It actively watches human behavior and reports directly to the Upper World, serving as an interactive substitute for a withdrawn creator.
figures.provenance: John R. Swanton, 'Social Organization and Social Usages of the Indians of the Creek Confederacy' (1928).

figures.gloss: The archetypal Mississippian martial hero, adorned in falcon regalia and immortalized on embossed copper plates. Linked to the Morning Star, he is the supernatural champion of the Upper World who plunges into the underworld to defeat chthonic monsters.
figures.facet: He fuses celestial avian grace with the brutal, head-taking realities of Mississippian warfare, validating the authority of elite warrior lineages — but the evidence for him is entirely iconographic (embossed copper plates, shell gorgets, engraved cups from Spiro and Moundville), a modern archaeological reconstruction (Reilly, Brown) rather than a narrated Muscogee oral tale.
figures.provenance: F. Kent Reilly III, 'Hero, Hawk, and Open Hand' (2004); James A. Brown, 'The Spiro Ceremonial Center' (1996).

figures.gloss: A massive, iridescent horned serpent residing in deep springs and subterranean caves, commanding the chaotic waters of the Underworld. It possesses a hypnotic allure and bears a glowing quartz crystal on its forehead, a talisman of immense power.
figures.facet: Sint-holo is deeply ambivalent rather than purely evil. It is a deadly aquatic hazard, yet also the ultimate source of shamanic power, forcing humans to bargain with chaos to secure success in hunting and war.
figures.provenance: George E. Lankford, 'Native American Legends of the Southeast' (1987).

figures.gloss: The irrepressible trickster who relies on fast-talking deception and sociopathic cunning to outwit larger predators. He is credited with stealing fire for humanity, even as he establishes cultural boundaries through his own disastrously foolish misadventures.
figures.facet: He possesses no divine majesty or martial strength, prevailing entirely through intelligence and manipulation. He embodies the survival tactics of the weak and serves as a vital, comic counterweight to the rigid martial honor of the chiefdoms.
figures.provenance: John R. Swanton, 'Myths and Tales of the Southeastern Indians' (1929).

figures.gloss: The primordial grandmother who secretly produced sustenance—corn and beans—from her own body. When her grandsons discovered her secret, they deemed her a witch and killed her, but by dragging her bleeding body across the earth, they spread agriculture to humanity.
figures.facet: Agriculture is born not as a sterile celestial gift, but through a tragic act of matricide and misunderstanding. The earth provides sustenance only because it was fertilized by the blood and sacrifice of the first mother.
figures.provenance: John R. Swanton, 'Myths and Tales of the Southeastern Indians' (1929), which records Muscogee/Creek and neighboring Southeastern versions of the slain corn-grandmother directly; James Mooney, 'Myths of the Cherokee' (1900), cited only as a regional Cherokee analogue.

figures.gloss: The foundational dualistic principle structuring Southeastern society, dividing towns, clans, and civic functions into 'White' (peace, law, sanctuary, old age) and 'Red' (war, kinetic action, youth, blood).
figures.facet: This is a strictly functional, complementary dualism, entirely divorced from moral concepts of good versus evil. The violent Red energy and the stabilizing White energy are both acknowledged as necessary, equal halves required to operate a balanced world.
figures.provenance: Charles Hudson, 'The Southeastern Indians' (1976); John R. Swanton (1928).

figures.gloss: The caffeinated yaupon-holly decoction drunk hot in the square ground to induce purgative purification before council and ceremony; served in whelk-shell cups whose trade reaches back to Mississippian times.
figures.facet: It supplies the biochemical technology beneath Poskita's moral reset: purgation is not metaphorical but literally induced, making bodily purity the precondition the community must meet before it can be judged clean.
figures.provenance: William Bartram, 'Travels' (1791); Charles M. Hudson (ed.), 'Black Drink: A Native American Tea' (1979); John R. Swanton (1928).

figures.gloss: Small forest-dwelling people who lead the lost astray or abduct and train future doctors, teaching plant medicine — feared, respected, and still spoken of.
figures.facet: They function as an informal ordination outside town office: a doctor's authority comes from having once been taken and taught by beings who live entirely outside the settled social order.
figures.provenance: John R. Swanton, 'Myths and Tales of the Southeastern Indians' (1929); George E. Lankford, 'Native American Legends of the Southeast' (1987).

figures.gloss: A witch who vomits up their internal organs at night to become a great horned owl and prey on human hearts; speaking of them was itself considered dangerous.
figures.facet: The witch's transformation is somatic and reversible-seeming rather than a simple shapeshift: the organs are set aside, not destroyed, which is precisely why finding and salting them was the standard countermeasure.
figures.provenance: John R. Swanton, 'Myths and Tales of the Southeastern Indians' (1929); documented among both Creek and Seminole tradition in George E. Lankford, 'Native American Legends of the Southeast' (1987).

figures.gloss: The account of the Creeks' emergence from the earth in the west, the crossing of rivers, the acquisition of sacred fire and war medicine, and the ranking of the towns, delivered by Chekilli to Oglethorpe in 1735 and preserved in translation.
figures.facet: Delivered orally to a colonial governor and surviving only in translation, it does double duty as origin narrative and 18th-century diplomacy in a single act of speech.
figures.provenance: Chekilli's speech to James Oglethorpe, Savannah, 1735 (Egmont Papers); published in Albert S. Gatschet, 'A Migration Legend of the Creek Indians' (1884); Swanton (1928).

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The supreme creator and his physical, earthly proxy work in tandem; the remote god breathes life, while the fire actively monitors and maintains local human order.
Intra-tradition: The archetypal Mississippian dichotomy of the martial, celestial avian warrior locked in eternal conflict with the chaotic, chthonic water serpent.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an incomprehensible, life-giving 'Great Mystery' that animates the cosmos without intervening in daily moral affairs.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are uncreated, supreme architects of truth and light who deploy sacred fire as their primary earthly manifestation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are conscious, sacred fires acting as the direct, earthly representatives of a remote, supreme celestial creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both personify the sacred ritual fire that consumes first-fruit offerings and acts as the crucial intermediary and witness between humans and the divine.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Shared structure of a vital annual festival that ritually vanquishes cosmic entropy and physically renews the world for another cyclical year.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are massive, socially binding ceremonies focused on world renewal, purification, and the cyclical resetting of communal time.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are foundational, multi-day harvest festivals centered on agricultural renewal, communal purification, and the ritual offering of the first crop.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Shared archetype of an apex avian predator serving as the fierce champion of the Upper World against deep-water subterranean monsters.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve celestial, monster-slaying champions who navigate the perilous underworld to secure martial order for the surface.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are formidable, horned underwater monsters that embody the chaotic peril of deep water across North American indigenous traditions.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are deeply feared, serpentine aquatic monsters associated with destructive storms and unpredictable natural violence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are culturally indispensable North American tricksters whose vanity and cunning paradoxically teach humanity survival and moral boundaries.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are animal tricksters who rely on deceit rather than physical strength to steal foundational resources—such as fire or light—for humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are physically weak but intensely intelligent tricksters who triumph over larger, stronger adversaries through rapid manipulation and sociopathy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both personify the foundational agricultural crops of the Eastern Woodlands as inherently maternal, sacred entities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose structural parallel of a divine feminine figure whose profound bodily suffering and reconfiguration ultimately grant life and agricultural civilization to humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize reality through an amoral, complementary dualism where opposing forces (kinetic/violent vs. still/peaceful) must be balanced to sustain cosmic order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the profound indigenous pursuit of systemic harmony and dynamic balance in civic and spiritual life, rather than a binary of good and evil.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: a rotating, calendrical renewal festival that re-binds separate communities to one another through shared ritual and sacrifice.
Intra-tradition: Asi is the required purifying element consumed during the Poskita before council and ceremony proceed.
Intra-tradition: the tradition's two classes of feared supernatural predator, one aerial-witch and one aquatic-serpent, both ambivalently bound up with shamanic power.
Intra-tradition: the charter narrative itself recounts the people's acquisition of the sacred fire.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: parallel Southeastern classes of hidden, immortal forest-people who lead travelers astray or intervene in human affairs from outside ordinary society.
Another North American Horned Serpent of springs and waters, structurally parallel to Kolowisi/Paalölöqangw; regional convergence, not proven descent.

Lankford's Southeastern horned-serpent complex treats these as one regional water-serpent under local names: Cherokee tradition centers the ulunsuti divination crystal cut from its brow, Muscogee tradition centers the tie-snake's binding power in war and hunting bargains.

One Southeastern rabbit-trickster complex shared by neighboring peoples and the documented root of Br'er Rabbit: Muscogee tellings foreground the fire-theft culture-bringer, Cherokee tellings the boastful dupe who loses as often as he wins.

Same slain-grandmother-of-corn narrative across the Southeast, with the Muscogee file's own provenance citing Mooney's Cherokee text as its regional analogue: Cherokee names her Selu, wife of Kanati, killed by the Wild Boy twins; Muscogee tellings leave her unnamed and make the killers her own grandsons.

dossier.essence: The religion of the Muscogee (Creek) people of the Southeastern Woodlands — today the Muscogee (Creek) Nation, Seminole, and related communities — together with its archaeological deep-background in the Mississippian mound-building cultures (c. 800–1600 CE) and the Southeastern Ceremonial Complex. It is a living tradition centered on the Master of Breath (Hisagita-imisi), the Sacred Fire, and the annual Green Corn ceremony (Poskita/Busk) of world renewal. Its distinctive frame divides the world into complementary Red (war) and White (peace) principles.
dossier.cosmos: The cosmos is tripartite: an orderly Upper World of sun, sky, and the falcon-warrior; a chaotic, fertile Under World of water and the horned serpent; and This World between them, where humans must keep the two apart and in balance. The Master of Breath, a remote sun-linked creator, animates all things with breath but leaves daily governance to his earthly proxy, the Sacred Fire, which watches human conduct. Time is cyclical, accumulating pollution that must be ritually annihilated and renewed each year.
dossier.practice: Religious life is organized around the tribal town (talwa) and its square ground, where the Sacred Fire is kindled at the center. The paramount rite is the Poskita (Busk): fasting, the purgative Black Drink (asi), amnesty for offenses short of murder, and the extinguishing and rekindling of all fires to reset the year. Stomp dances, the ballgame ('little brother of war'), matrilineal clans, and the Red/White town division structure ceremonial and civic order; ceremonial grounds remain active today.
dossier.canon: An oral tradition — migration legends (such as the Kasihta/Cussita legend recorded in 1735), sacred formulas, and ceremonial knowledge — read alongside the material iconographic record of Mississippian art (engraved copper plates and shell gorgets from Etowah, Spiro, and elsewhere).
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds annual world-renewal — a cosmos that accumulates disorder and must be ritually reset to zero — bound to a purity logic in which the categories of order and chaos must be kept scrupulously apart.
sources: John R. Swanton, 'Religious Beliefs and Medical Practices of the Creek Indians' (1928)
George E. Lankford, 'Reachable Stars: Patterns in the Ethnoastronomy of Eastern North America' (2007)
F. Kent Reilly III & James F. Garber (eds.), 'Ancient Objects and Sacred Realms: Interpretations of Mississippian Iconography' (2007)
